summary:
Claim: Annealing is primarily a stress-relieving heat treatment.  
Evidence: - Across materials science, reducing internal stresses is the core purpose of annealing. - Even when other effects like grain refinement occur, stress relief defines the process fundamentally.  
Answer: D

The claim identifies the primary function of annealing as stress relief, aligning with standard definitions in materials science. This distinguishes it from alternative interpretations focusing on microstructural changes alone. Key evidence supports this through two points: first, internal stress reduction is consistently cited as the foundational goal of annealing regardless of material type or application; second, although secondary benefits such as improved ductility or grain size may result from specific annealing processes, they are not the defining feature—the fundamental mechanism remains stress mitigation via controlled heating and cooling cycles. Thus, despite potential variations among subtypes (e.g., full vs partial annealing), all share the common intent of relieving residual or work-induced stresses. Option D encapsulates this essence most accurately by emphasizing stress relief without overemphasizing incidental outcomes. In contrast, options suggesting only structural modification or property enhancement fail to reflect the universal applicability and centrality of stress management in annealing practices. Hence, D stands out as both precise and inclusive within the broader scope of metallurgical treatments.
